# Additively Manufacturable High-Strength Aluminum Alloys with Coarsening-Resistant Microstructures Achieved via Rapid Solidification

S. Mohadeseh Taheri-Mousavi,* Michael Xu, Florian Hengsbach, Clay Houser, Zhaoxuan Ge, Benjamin Glaser, Shaolou Wei, Mirko Schaper, James M. LeBeau, Greg B. Olson, and A. John Hart*

Additively manufactured aluminum (Al) alloys with high strength have broad industrial applications. Strength promotion necessitates a high-volume fraction of small, closely spaced precipitates to effectively impede dislocation motion. Here, it is shown that for certain compositions in the Al-Er-Zr-Y-Yb-Ni alloy class, L1₂-Al₃M phases, the primary strength contributor, can initially precipitate as submicron-scale ( $ \approx $100 nm) metastable ternary phases under the rapid solidification of powder bed additive manufacturing; yet the subsequent coarsening-resistant L1₂-Al₃M phases that precipitate during heat treatment remain at the nanometer scale, imparting high strength. A candidate alloy is designed using hybrid calculation of phase diagrams (CALPHAD)-based integrated computational materials engineering (ICME) and Bayesian optimization algorithms. Powder is manufactured for this alloy and is additively manufactured into crack-free macroscale specimens with a strength that is five-fold that of the equivalent cast alloy and comparable to wrought Al 7075. After aging at 400 °C for 8 h, the room-temperature tensile strength reaches 395 MPa, which is 50% stronger than the best-known benchmark printable Al alloy. This integrated computational-experimental workflow shows the considerable potential to exploit rapid solidification in additive manufacturing to design alloys with commercially deployable properties.

## 1. Introduction

Additively manufactured (AM) aluminum (Al) alloys with high strength at elevated service temperatures (e.g.,  $ \approx $150–400 °C) can find broad industrial applications in components of jet engines, combustion engines, vacuum pumps, and more. $ ^{[1]} $ Laser-based AM, particularly laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) that operates under non-equilibrium conditions due to rapid solidification, can endow the alloy products with microstructural features that are challenging to attain via conventional near-equilibrium liquid metallurgy routes. One of the outstanding benefits of rapid solidification is the metastable phases that can effectively modulate the macroscopic physical and/or mechanical properties of as-built or heat-treated alloys.

For example, metastable  $ Al_{6}Fe $ phases in AM Al-Fe alloys have led to simultaneous improvement in thermal conductivity and strength. $ ^{[2]} $ Moreover, AM of

Al-Ce-Mg alloys promotes a metastable ternary  $ Al_{23}CeMg_{6} $ phase, leading to higher strength.[3] Similar mechanical benefits have also been reported for  $ \theta^{\prime} $- $ Al_{2}Cu $ in cast and AM Al-Cu-Mn-Zr alloys.[4] In Al 2024, metastable  $ L1_{2} $- $ Al_{3}Ti $ due to rapid solidification, instead of stable  $ D0_{22} $- $ Al_{3}Ti $, leads to improvement in both strength and ductility.[5] Similarly, metastable  $ L1_{2} $- $ Al_{3}Zr $ instead of brittle  $ D0_{23} $- $ Al_{3}Zr $ in Al-Mg-Zr alloys induces noticeable precipitation strengthening as well as promoting grain refinement and inhibiting solidification cracking.[6] Finally, in AM Al-Ce-Mn alloys ( $ Al_{20}Mn_{2}Ce $),  $ Al_{6}Mn $ increases as-built hardness and transforms to  $ Al_{11}Ce_{3} $ and  $ Al_{12}Mn $ after aging, contributing to further increased hardness.[7] These and other examples show that rapid solidification can be exploited to trigger precipitation of metastable phases to unravel new routes for alloy design.

Three microstructural conditions are critical in designing Al alloys with high mechanical strength at elevated temperatures.

1) Thermodynamic stability over a broad temperature window. Two strategies are commonly employed to strengthen Al alloys $ ^{1} $: employing precipitates, especially with L1 $ _{2} $ structures, is the first main strategy, $ ^{[8]} $ and segmenting grains by laminated eutectic phases to further impede dislocation motion $ ^{1} $. Following the first strategy that is the focus of this study, strength has an inverse relationship with the precipitate size based on Orowan strengthening theory. $ ^{[8]} $ Thus, for a given volume fraction of precipitates, smaller precipitate size and spacing more effectively impede dislocation motion. Although laser AM generally leads to smaller precipitates due to short growth time during rapid solidification, precipitate sizes usually grow to the micrometer scale, especially as their volume fraction increases. Here, we show that for tailored compositions of Al alloys, rapid solidification can cause precipitation of metastable ternary phases at submicron-meter scales (Figures 1–3). The subsequent heat treatment of printed material transforms ternary phases into L1 $ _{2} $-Al $ _{3} $M (referred as L1 $ _{2} $ in this paper) and Al $ _{3} $Ni. Importantly, L1 $ _{2} $ phases remain at the nanometer scale even at high volume fractions.

2) Sluggish coarsening kinetics. Sluggish coarsening kinetics occur if the strengthening phases do not grow and the fraction of fast-growing phases is minimized. These two factors were considered in the present alloy design (Figure 4a,f, Results and Discussion: Step IV). To this end, L1₂ structures are preferred due to their coherent interfaces with the Al matrix. Certain elements in the periodic table promote L1₂ formation during casting or rapid solidification of AM. As discussed in Discussion S1 (Supporting Information), it is typically necessary to add heavy high-melting-point elements, which results in macro-segregation during casting. This engineering challenge thus limits the maximum concentration of each element. Layer-wise melting of laser AM, fortunately, circumvents this challenge.

3) Resistance to climb-mediated creep deformation. This is achieved via controlling vacancy diffusion or tailoring the length scales of microstructural features.

Besides all conditions for high-temperature strengthening, it is required that the Al alloys be printable (defined as remaining

Processing defects can be minimized by optimization of processing parameters. However, solidification defects originate from the solidification nature of the material, e.g., the time and temperature change, or the number of grain nucleation sources. For example, a widely used high-strength Al alloy, Al 7075, cannot readily be printed without modification to its composition as it is prone to hot cracking (Figure 5c; Figure S20, Supporting Information). $ ^{[9,10]} $

Through an integrated computational-experimental workflow, this study addresses the first two conditions and results in a family of alloys that do not exhibit hot cracking. To inhibit hot cracking in the sample, the temperature range and solidification time of the designed alloy were controlled. As the dimensions of the compositional space (here, five dimensions) and the number of objectives increase, defining the critical parameters governing the microstructural features and, ultimately, the desired engineering properties becomes significantly challenging, motivating the use of machine learning (ML) techniques (Figure 5a; Figure S1, Supporting Information). The design approach was demonstrated by performing a full alloy design cycle of a model Al alloy (Al-Er-Zr-Y-Yb-Ni) for AM, spanning from virtual predictions to experimental validation (Figure 5a). The fine distribution of metastable ternary phases (Al-Ni-M, M = Er, Zr, Y, Yb) through rapid solidification serves as the source for the reactive elements, enabling nano-meter scale precipitation of a high phase percentage of L1₂-Al₃M (M = Er, Zr, Y, Yb) strengthening phases (Figures 1 and 3). ## 2. Results and Discussion

CALPHAD-based ICME simulations were combined with ML techniques to efficiently explore the design space and find non-linear correlations among elemental concentrations (Figure 5b). Predicted mechanical properties were compared to the best-known benchmark Al alloy, which has been designed within the same compositional space and target properties, yet without ML techniques. For initial rapid validation of the designed compositions, promising candidates were induction melted and laser scanned to mimic the conditions of AM without requiring custom powder formulation. The top candidate alloy, Al-0.4Er-1Zr-1.33Ni (Al-2.33Er-3.19Zr-2.73Ni wt.%), was found to have 50% higher hardness (147 HV) after aging at 400 °C for 8 h than the benchmark alloy (100 HV for ‘alloy 1’, Al-1.35Er -1.15Zr-0.25Y-0.73Yb-2.14Ni, wt.%), which was prepared by the same method (Figure 1). The microstructure and properties of two other control alloys were also demonstrated to elucidate the design concept. As seen in Figure 1, slower solidification via induction melting (casting) leads to micro-meter scale ternary and Al₃M phases (Figure S15, Supporting Information) and 80% lower hardness (40 HV) than the 3D-printed sample (200 HV in Figure S17, Supporting Information).

An Al alloy with an identical molar percentage of Er and Zr but without Ni was also made. In this alloy  $ L_{1} $ phases directly form during rapid solidification. The hardness of this control sample was measured (W/O ternary in Figure 1), and its microstructure was analyzed (yellow arrow in Figure 1; Figure S16, Supporting Information). (SEM)-based energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) characterization shows several large clusters of micrometer-scale Zr-rich phases within the grain interiors. These clusters are not present in the top-designed alloy. This composition has a hardness of 110 HV, which is 34% lower than the designed composition containing submicron-meter scale ternary phases processed in an identical procedure.

Powder was then produced from the alloy that exhibited the highest hardness, and specimens were fabricated by LPBF AM, enabling hardness measurements, tensile tests, and microstructure characterization to be performed. After aging (8 h, 400 °C), hardness (200 HV) is once more 50% higher than the benchmark alloy (133 HV), and comparable to wrought Al 7075 (Figure S17, Supporting Information). The high strength and the coarsening resistance of the designed alloy at elevated temperatures were further confirmed by performing room temperature tensile tests on as-built and aged samples (Figure 6). The peak tensile strength after 8 h of aging is 395 MPa, which is likewise ≈50% higher than the benchmark printable Al alloy $ ^{11} $ (275 HV) and is maintained without significant decrease after aging for 48 h at 400 °C. In what follows, the design procedure for these alloys and then a reflection on further improvements in properties achieved by refinement of the design are described.

##### Step I: Extracting microstructural features

To realize an alloy composition that exhibits high-temperature strength and printability, microstructural features controlling these properties were first identified. During solidification of the Al alloy system (Al-Er-Zr-Y-Yb-Ni), several precipitates form, including D0₂₃-Al₃Zr (referred as Al₃Zr in this paper), L1₂, Al₃Ni, and metastable ternary phase Al₂₃Ni₆M₄. Al₃Zr is brittle and undermines strength and ductility;¹¹] its lower solvus temperature and phase percentage minimize this phase. These microstructural features can be quantified through single equilibrium calculations (Figure 4d,e). L1₂ is stable at high temperature (Figure S3, Supporting Information) and contributes to strengthening by impeding dislocations.[⁸] At low temperature, based on the Orowan strengthening mechanism, dislocations bow around precipitates and leave behind dislocation loops. Based on Equation (1) (see Methods Section, σ_or ∝  $ \frac{1}{R} $), the shear stress required for these mechanisms is inversely proportional to the precipitate size R, above the shear to bypass critical size of ≈2 nm. The goal is to achieve such a nanometer-scale dispersion after aging, and at elevated service temperature. A high content of L1₂ phase at 250 °C without formation during solidification (low L1₂ phase percentage at 660 °C, which is the melting temperature of pure Al) maximizes the concentration of nanoscale precipitates. These percentages were determined through single equilibrium calculations (Figure 4b,c). It is noteworthy that metastable precipitates such as Al₂₃Ni₆M₄, in which M can potentially be Er, Zr, Y, or Yb, may form at sub-micrometer scales during rapid solidification, working as efficient reactant reservoirs for the L1₂ precipitates during aging. The volume fractions of these precipitates after solidification must be maximized and are calculated from non-equilibrium Scheil solidification simulations (Figure 4f).

To maintain strength at higher temperatures, a coarsening-resistant microstructure is required. Thus, the coarsening of L1₂ precipitates and the volume fraction of rapidly coarsening Al₃Ni must be minimized (Figure 4a,f). Based on Equation (2) in the Methods Section, for the coarsening rate of phases in multi-component alloying systems (k ∝  $ \frac{V_{m}^{\beta} \sigma}{\sum_{i=2}^{N} (\bar{C}_{i}^{\beta} - \bar{C}_{i}^{\alpha})^{2} / M_{i}} $), [12] three factors are of mechanistic significance to decrease the coarsening rate: (1) low atomic mobility (M₁); (2) reduced interfacial energy (σ, mediated by coherency); and (3) enhanced oversaturation (C₁β - C₁α)². Reducing the interphase misfit strain (ε = 100% | 1 -  $ \frac{a}{a_0} $|) (Equation 3 in the Methods Section) is crucial for minimizing the interfacial energy of semi-/coherent L1₂ phases, where a and a₀ are the lattice parameters of the phase and Al

matrix, respectively. Therefore, the interfacial energy was substituted with misfit strain as  $ (k \propto \frac{\text{Misfit strain}}{\sum_{i=2}^{N} (\bar{C}_{i}^{\beta} - \bar{C}_{i}^{\alpha})^{2} / M_{i}}) $, and considered as the coarsening metric. This metric can be computed from simulations for different compositions. A previous study of L1₂ strengthened Al alloys has shown that long-term creep strength controlled by interfacial climb is defined with a threshold stress of 40% of Orowan strength (Equation 1 in the Methods Section) for particles with an optimum size of 17 nm diameter.[13] Therefore, it is important to achieve an initial dispersion below this optimum size. With the defined attributes to achieve high temperature strengthening (Figure 5a; Figure S1, Supporting Information), next, the descriptors to achieve printability were defined.

The LPBF process can be considered as a multi-layer microwelding process. The main mode of fracture during solidification of Al alloys is hot tearing/cracking, which limits the printability and weldability of many Al alloys such as those in the widely used 6000 and 7000 series. $ ^{[14]} $ Hot cracking can therefore be prevented by constraining the solidification characteristics. This includes limiting the freezing range (FR) and the cracking susceptibility coefficient (CSC) of Clyne and Davies, defined by solidification time ratio $ ^{[15]} $ (Figure S11a,b, Supporting Information). The CSC is best limited by introducing a small fraction of eutectic solidification. The overall hot cracking susceptibility (HCS) was taken as the product of FR and CSC, $ ^{[15]} $ both obtained from Scheil calculations $ ^{[11]} $ (Figure S11c, Supporting Information). In all, the material descriptors are the phase percentages and size of all phases, the coarsening metric of L1 $ _{2} $ phases, and the three solidification parameters (FR, CSC, and HCS), as summarized in Figure S1 (Supporting Information).

## Step II: High-throughput simulations and simple data analytics

To estimate the aforementioned material descriptors, sets of 250,000 and 500,000 random compositions were generated using the Latin hypercube sampling (LHS) method (Figure S4, Supporting Information), with the following constraints on concentrations of elements as:  $ \{Er, Zr\} = [0, 2], \{Y, Yb\} = [0, 1] $, and  $ \{Ni\} = [0, 4] $ at.\%. Note that the units for elemental concentration and phase content are at.\% and mol%, respectively, except otherwise noted. The ranges were chosen based on previous experience and to aim for dilute concentrations, $ ^{[11]} $ and were the inputs for all estimations. The elements were selected based on their potential ability to form  $ L1_{2} $ and metastable phases, their solubility and mobility in Al matrix, and their cost (Discussion S1, Supporting Information). $ ^{[11]} $

Two primary microstructural features that need to be minimized are the inverse of diffusion resistivity (the denominator of Equation 2, Methods Section) and the misfit strain of the L1₂ phases; as described above, the product is considered as the coarsening metric  $ \frac{\text{Misfit strain}}{\sum_{i=2}^{N} (\bar{C}_i^\beta - \bar{C}_i^\alpha)^2 / M_i} $. Figure S4 (Supporting Information) shows the diffusion resistivity, misfit strain, and coarsening metric at 250 °C, all normalized by the associated values from the benchmark alloy. Among 250 000 sampled compositions, 28.8% have a lower coarsening metric than the benchmark alloy. The lowest predicted coarsening metric among this population is 2.08 times that of the benchmark alloy (Figure 5b), and among the population of 500 000 the maximum is 2.50 times. See Discussion S2 (Supporting Information) for more elaboration on these compositions and associated figures (Figures S4–S8, Supporting Information).



Zr and Er are the most influential elements on the coarsening metric (Figures S5–S7, Supporting Information and Discussion S2, Supporting Information). This qualitative analysis was confirmed by the Spearman coefficient, an index indicating linear correlation for each ranked pair of parameters (Figure 7; Discussion S3, Supporting Information). Increasing Zr decreases coarsening metric as it has the highest change of solubility in L1₂ phase and Al matrix and also lowest diffusivity in Al matrix. Er has the most negative index. Strength is also correlated to the L1₂ content (see Equation 1, Methods Section); Er is the most influential element increasing this percentage based on the Spearman coefficient analysis (Figure S8, Supporting Information). While cracking susceptibility is not strongly correlated with composition, FR is strongly correlated with Er and Yb, where increasing Er and reducing Yb lowers FR. The Yb influence can be related to the behavior of critical ternary phases, as lowering Yb similarly promotes the formation of the metastable ternary phase. Y and Ni have the highest influence on ternary phase percentages. This was further confirmed by a comprehensive study of the influence of Y on ternary phase formation.[16] While this information shows the influence of elements on different microstructural features, it cannot capture non-linear effects. ML algorithms are therefore needed to account for the non-linear effects.

##### Step III: Develop ML surrogate models

The coarsening metric was chosen as a primary microstructural feature, and the effects of various elements on this descriptor were then studied. To develop a surrogate model, six regression techniques were compared: a neural network (NN), K-nearest neighbor (KNN), random forest (RF), support vector machine (SVR), gradient boost (GB), and linear regression (LR) (Figure S2, Supporting Information). The input vector for the regressors contains the elemental concentration of five alloying elements. Each of these regressors has several hyperparameters that need to be optimized, and 5-fold cross-validation was used to perform the optimization. The process of optimizing the hyperparameters for each technique is described in the Methods Section. The test data root mean square error (RMSE) was analyzed for different training data percentages. As shown in Figure S2 (Supporting Information), the normalized prediction error for the most efficient algorithm, NN, decreases to 3% as the training set comprises 40 compositions (0.01% of data). NN thus requires the lowest amount of data to achieve the lowest error in prediction. Comparing the high difference of NN errors compared to linear regression (0.2 vs 20) clearly shows that there are non-linear effects that influence the coarsening metrics. The NN surrogate model, which connects elemental concentrations to coarsening metrics reliably, was then used as a forward prediction model.

##### Step IV: Inverse design of alloy composition

Next, alloy compositions were inversely designed to optimize descriptors governing high temperature strengthening while maintaining printability (Figure 5a). This is the most critical step in the design as it reveals the influential elements on each property and on the combinations of properties while considering non-linear effects. The results can be compared with those in

Figure 7. Heat map indicating the Spearman coefficients between each pair of parameters in the design space of the model alloy Al-Er-Zr-Y-Yb-Ni. The descriptors are calculated from high-throughput single equilibrium or Scheil simulations. Coefficients demonstrate strong dependence of metastable ternary phases on Ni and Y concentrations, while the additions of Zr and Er are most strongly correlated with low coarsening metric and high phase fractions of  $ L1_{2} $ phase, respectively. FR and HCS depend strongly on Er and Yb concentrations.

Step II to highlight nonlinearities. Bayesian optimization (BO) with a Gaussian process as the surrogate model was used to perform inverse design. The sampling was done using LHS at each step. To balance exploration and exploitation, expected improvement (EI) was chosen as the acquisition function. Further details are provided in the Methods Section.

The first strategy to solve this problem is to maximize performance and then check if printability metrics are satisfied as well. Thus, initial tests of the BO schema focused on objective functions related to mechanical performance, i.e., targeting a high content of the L1₂ phase, a small size of this phase, and a low coarsening metric compared to the benchmark alloy. Some of the tested objective functions were maximization of (1/Coarsening metric) and (L1₂/Coarsening metric), followed by integrating the increase in the difference between L1₂ content from single equilibrium versus Scheil calculations to reduce the scale of the phase,  $ ((\mathrm{L1}_{2}^{\mathrm{Single}} - \mathrm{L1}_{2}^{\mathrm{Scheil}})/\mathrm{Coarsening} $ metric).

For example, using the first objective function, it was discovered that Y and Yb must be zero. Ni has no influence as for each run, BO proposed a new value for the Ni. The coarsening metric is controlled by Er and Zr. High Zr content was also predicted by simple data analytics (Spearman coefficients, Figure 7). However, high Er concentration is in contradiction to what was concluded above. As seen in Figure 7, the high Er concentration is due to the influence of Er on  $ L1_{2} $ content. If Er is not present, all Zr would precipitate as brittle  $ Al_{3}Zr $ phases, which are detrimental to strength and ductility. Er thus has non-linear effects on the coarsening metric by generating a high content of  $ L1_{2} $ phases, such that Zr can dissolve into  $ L1_{2} $ and decrease the coarsening metrics. Zr contributes to lowering coarsening metrics due to its high solubility in  $ L1_{2} $ and relatively low diffusivity in Al, and also low misfit strain (see Equation 2 in Methods Section). The objective functions that contain  $ L1_{2} $ concentration guide us to the high concentration of Er to achieve the goal, confirming the Spearman coefficients.

Meantime, the objective function containing the difference of  $ L1_{2} $ concentrations of a single equilibrium with the Scheil suggests that again Er is the main influential element on this objective. Thus, the inverse of the coarsening metric (1/Coarsening metric) was considered as the objective function, together with considering a constraint for Er concentration to minimize  $ L1_{2} $ concentration in Scheil calculations. This was in fact the best

objective function to explore as it resulted in the ML-optimized composition with 40% enhancement in  $ L1_{2} $ content versus the benchmark alloy, as well as a 3.5 times (71%) reduction in the coarsening metric. The ML-optimized alloy met all requirements for printability, and all  $ L1_{2} $ phases were predicted to initially precipitate as ternary phases. Therefore, the ML-optimized alloy was a valid design from all aspects. However, the search was continued with the hope to find compositions with higher enhancements in all descriptors and properties. Two additional strategies were thus tried, and printability metrics alone and their combinations with mechanical performances were considered as design objectives, respectively. Further details are presented in the Methods Section.

The first strategy was adopted, and thus minimizing the coarsening metric was defined as the primary goal. Constraints were also considered on Er and Zr concentrations to control the size and phase percentages of  $ L1_{2} $ and  $ Al_{3}Zr $ phases, respectively (Figure 5a; Figure S1, Supporting Information). For these calculations, for example, to obtain the minimum coarsening metric, the BO algorithm was connected directly to ICME techniques (i.e., calculating the coarsening metric after running ThermoCalc software through a TC-Python script, see Methods Section), and the optimal composition emerged after 4 rounds of sampling with 20 samples in a single batch (80 data). To verify the efficiency of the BO to find the global minimum for this metric, its efficiency was also compared with particle swarm optimization (PSO), which is a constrained optimization technique, as an alternative approach. The data was generated using the NN surrogate model. The minimum coarsening metric was identical to the one determined from BO for the composition with XNi2Er2Zr. Here, XNi indicates Ni does not influence the coarsening metric. The search converged after 8 steps of 10 sampling data for each step (80 sample data in total).

The BO was also rerun but this time the data was not obtained from direct ICME techniques, and instead was obtained through the NN surrogate model. Notably, if data is obtained through the NN surrogate model, only simulations for 40 data (composition as an input and coarsening metric as a label) to train NN model are the main computational cost (Figure 5b). For BO, the algorithm converged after 100 samples. Interestingly, this shows that the initial generation of the surrogate model (required only 40 samples compared to 80 without the surrogate model), was useful in reducing the number of the required data. Yet in all cases, the data generated from simulations is much less costly than experimental data, and the comparison shows the efficiency of alternative algorithms in finding extremums, which also depend on the objective functions. The normalized coarsening metric of the optimal composition is 3.5 times (71%) lower than the benchmark alloy, reinforcing the power of inverse design techniques compared to high-throughput calculations with random sampling, which achieved only 2.5 times (60%) lower after 0.5 M data generation) (Figure 5b). Further details regarding the inverse design techniques are given in the Methods Section.

As the coarsening metric is now mainly governed by Er and Zr, the problem is reduced to a 2D space. A contour plot of the normalized coarsening metric with respect to the Er and Zr concentrations (Figure 4a) shows that both are required for improvement in the metric. $ ^{[17]} $ Contour plots of other microstructural features with respect to these two elements are also presented. The contour plots of  $ Al_{3}Zr $ phase percentage and  $ Al_{3}Zr $ solvus temperature, which limit maximum Zr concentration, appear in Figure 4d,e. The maximum concentration of Er limits the ultimate  $ L1_{2} $ phase fraction and also the  $ L1_{2} $ content during solidification. We need to increase the phase fraction while the radii remain low (Figure 4b,c). The maximum Er concentration was chosen to be equal to 0.4 at.% to limit the  $ L1_{2} $ content during solidification to 1 (Figure 4c). The Zr concentration was also considered to be 1. The uncertainty of both  $ L1_{2} $ phase percentage and coarsening metric was then quantified, and it was shown that even with the change of elemental concentration up to 50%, these two descriptors will not change more than 5% with 99% confidence (Figure S10, Supporting Information). Details of these analyses are provided in Discussion S4 (Supporting Information).

The foregoing single equilibrium calculations predict microstructural features after aging. However, consistent with the experimental observations, non-equilibrium Scheil solidification simulations with our database revealed that the stabilized L1₂ precipitates can appear as a submicron-meter scale metastable ternary phase Al₂₃Ni₆M₄ during solidification. The ternary phase acts as a reservoir for the solid-state precipitation of nano-meter scale L1₂ during aging at elevated temperatures. Therefore, the Ni content needs to be optimized to give a higher percentage of the ternary phase for a given Er and Zr. For Zr concentration equal to 1 at.%, all the phases previously mentioned from Scheil calculations with respect to Ni concentration are plotted in Figure 4f. This shows that initially at Ni = 0, the ternary and L1₂ contents are zero and a maximum of 1.63, respectively. As Ni increases, the ternary phase percentage increases to a maximum value of 3.21 at 0.64 of Ni. Thus, Ni must be > 0.64 while further addition of Ni increases the amount of unnecessary Al₃Ni.

In the next stage, the above-mentioned printability constraints of the design are examined in detail. With Er = 0.4, for the variation range of Zr and Ni, CSC, FR, and HCS products were plotted (Figure S11, Supporting Information). The HCS product was very low (< 0.05) for all compositions (compared to 1.13 for no-printable Al 7075), indicating good printability within the composition constraints.

## Step V: Rapid experimental workflow to evaluate mechanical properties and printability

Next, a rapid experimental workflow was developed to validate the predictions for performance and printability in the context of rapid solidification (Discussion S5, Supporting Information). A matrix of nine compositions was chosen surrounding the predicted optimum (indicated by diamond points in Figure S11, Supporting Information). Small-scale samples of the nine selected compositions were first fabricated by induction melting, and then we mimicked the heating and solidification conditions of AM on these samples by scanning laser melting. The prediction was validated with no cracks observed after solidification for all nine samples. The sample with Ni = 1.33 and Zr = 1 is shown in Figure 5c for a single laser path. Laser scanning on Al 7075 was also performed which shows clear hot cracking in association with its known poor printability (Figure 5c).

Multiple path laser scanning mimicking printing one layer of the powder was also performed, and no cracks were found (Figure S18, Supporting Information). Comparisons of multi-scale microstructural features between laser-scanned and AM

samples were provided in our recent study. $ ^{[18]} $ As shown in detail in the study of Ge et al., $ ^{[19]} $ 79% of the strength at service temperature of this alloy is governed by nano-meter scale  $ L_{12} $ precipitates. The main conclusion from the comprehensive study on 3D-pritned and laser scanned samples was that while some differences are observed in some characteristics of ternary,  $ Al_{3}Ni $, and  $ Al_{3}Zr $ phases at different scales, the scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) analyses show that the  $ L_{12} $ phases have identical length scale and composition in both samples. Both  $ L_{12} $ phases have a similar core shell structure for Er and Zr distribution as shown in Figure 3l,m for the AM sample.

To perform quantitative analyses on the density of solidification defects in laser-scanned samples, X-ray computed tomography (CT)-scan of the two samples with highest HCS were conducted and compared with the scanned Al 7075 sample. The samples with a composition of Al-0.4Er-1Zr-1.33Ni and Al-0.4Er-2Zr-0Ni were thus chosen (Figure S11c, Supporting Information). The two samples and an Al 7075 sheet were scanned with identical parameters as described in the Methods Section (Figure S20a–c, Supporting Information). In the CT images, the scanned layers are distinguished by their darker contrast compared to the cast substrate. No cracks or pores were detected throughout the scanned volumes of both samples. However, the Al 7075 sample contains a large density of low aspect ratio defects as shown from quantitative analyses in Figure S20d,e (Supporting Information). Here, the aspect ratio was calculated from short axes/long axes of defects. It is seen that the low aspect ratio defects are attributed to long cracks over each laser track. The density of defects in the scanned area was calculated as 1.43%. While CT scanning provides more detailed analyses of the crack density compared to optical microscopy in Figure 5c, however, for hot cracks, optical microscopy images are a sufficiently rapid screening method to downselect a composition for printability as performing each CT scan and its associated analyses took 5.5 h.

Next, to assess the strengthening behavior, Vickers hardness measurements on these nine samples were performed in both the as-built condition and after aging (8 h, 400 °C). Figure S12 (Supporting Information) shows the contour plot of hardness for these nine samples at these conditions. Consistent with the predicted optimum composition, the sample with Ni = 1.33 and Zr = 1 showed the highest increase in hardness with aging, as expected from L1₂ phase precipitation replacing the metastable ternary phase. The aged hardness of 147 HV is 50% higher than that of the benchmark alloy, which was processed and tested under identical conditions. Moreover, this hardness is 34% higher than the sample with Zr = 1 and Ni = 0, which does not contain ternary phases due to the lack of Ni. The SEM-EDS image of this sample shows several clusters of micro-meter scale Zr-rich areas inside grains that are much scarce in the sample with Zr = 1 and Ni = 1.33 (Figure S16, Supporting Information). Detailed characterizations of these clusters are required for more comprehensive design insights and are considered as future research.

## Step VI: 3D printing and characterization of microstructure and nanoprecipitates

Based on this validation, Al-0.4Er-1Zr-1.33Ni (Al-2.33Er-3.19Zr-2.73Ni wt.%) was chosen as the optimal alloy candidate for powder manufacturing by ultrasonic atomization. Based on the uncertainty quantification analyses and as seen in Figure 4a, the coarsening metric of this composition is relatively stable even if the Er or Zr contents change by 50%. The 3D printing process parameters, namely the power and scan speed, were optimized to achieve the highest density as described in Discussion S6, Supplementary Information. Samples  $ (28 \times 6 \times 6 $ and  $ 6 \times 6 \times 6 $ mm $ ^{3} $, Figure 6a) were verified to be crack-free. EBSD images from the side and top of the sample show non-textured equiaxed grains, comparable to the microstructure achieved in other studies with addition of nanoparticle nucleants. $ ^{[14]} $ (Figure 6b). Vickers hardness tests parallel to the build direction and on top of the samples were conducted in the as-built condition and after isothermal aging at 400 °C. These values are compared in Figure S17 (Supporting Information) with the AM benchmark Al alloy and Al 7075 aged under identical conditions. In the as-built condition, the sample exhibits 180 HV, comparable to wrought Al 7075. At peak hardness after 8–10 h aging, the designed alloy has higher hardness than Al 7075 and ≈50% higher hardness (200 HV) than the AM benchmark alloy (133 HV). The hardness has a similar gradual decrease as the benchmark alloy. Moreover, the hardness always surpasses values from wrought Al 7075.



The foregoing designed alloy was also processed by induction melting. An SEM-EDS image of this sample shows large micrometer size ternary phases (Figure S15, Supporting Information). The hardness of this sample is 5 times (80%) smaller than the aged AM sample, which further confirms our concept of alloy design to exploit rapid solidification to establish submicron-meter scale ternary phases for strength enhancement (Figure 1). As mentioned, besides precipitation strengthening, the main strategy which is used in Al alloys is exploiting laminated eutectic phases to segment grains. $ ^{[20]} $ This strategy has a significant contribution for the strengthening, especially as AM processing decreases the length scales of lamellas to the nanometer scale. $ ^{[21]} $ As seen in Figure 6c, the designed alloy, which only exploits precipitation hardening has higher strength than top two designs (benchmark alloy & Al-Ce-Mn), which exploited both strengthening mechanisms. $ ^{[7,11]} $ To further confirm the enhanced strength, tensile tests at room temperatures on samples aged for different aging times at 400 °C were performed. As shown in Figure 6c, the high strength and its stability are also confirmed in these experiments. Once more, the sample has 395 MPa strength, which is 50% higher yield strength than that of Gong et al.'s benchmark printable Al alloy. $ ^{[11]} $

Microstructural characterization was performed by SEM, STEM, and local electrode atom probe (LEAP) atom probe tomography (APT) on the printed material aged for 8 h at 400 °C, representing the peak hardness condition. At low magnification, melt pool boundaries and grains can be detected (Figures 8e and S13a, Supporting Information), and the primary Al₃Ni phase is observed uniformly throughout the sample (Figure S13b, Supporting Information). One large Zr-rich precipitate is also observed at this scale. The grain size and aspect ratio distributions from this image are presented in Figure 8c,d. At higher magnification, at which grains and the precipitates on grain boundaries are visible (Figure 8a), the EDS map (Figure 8b) more visibly indicates large-scale Ni- and Zr-rich areas consistent with predicted primary Al₃Ni and Al₃Zr phases, respectively. Ternary phases are identified when Er and Ni are observed together in EDS maps.

Figure 8. a) SEM image of the ML-optimized 3D-printed sample (aged at 400 °C for 8 h) shows various shading (phases) in the microstructure. b) EDS image of SEM image showing elemental distribution. c) Grain size and d) aspect ratio distribution from an EBSD image e). The EBSD image is adapted from ref.[19] The average values are indicated on the figures.

and are mainly at grain boundaries of grains that are inside melt pools.

At higher SEM magnification for a field of view located near a melt pool boundary, Al₃Ni (yellow N) and Zr-rich phases (red Z) are present (Figure S13c, Supporting Information). Based on TEM analyses from several previous studies at a similar length scale, the Zr-rich precipitates may have D0₂₃ or L1₂ structures.[²²-²⁵] However, it was reported that if the precipitate has a well-defined cubic morphology, consistent with their coherent crystal structure with the Al lattice, and also located in the middle of grains, the TEM analyses mainly evidenced an L1₂ structure. These primary precipitates most likely served as nucleation sites that promoted the formation of fine, equiaxed grains during solidification. Their distribution and morphology play a critical role in grain refinement and enhancing the printability and mechanical performance of AM Al alloys. More SEM analyses at this resolution for samples with different aging conditions (as-built, 8 h, and 48 h) and from undeformed and deformed samples were reported in our previous study.[¹⁹] The size distributions of phases are adapted from this study and presented in Figure S13e–g (Supporting Information).

TEM images present higher resolution identification of Al₃Ni and especially ternary phases, showing that the latter is unique for the current design. The secondary phases at the grain boundaries are segmented from the atomic-number sensitive high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) overview (Figure 2a, field of view of 3.2 μm × 4.6 μm) using binary thresholding and morphological filtering, with the particles in the resulting mask identified based on contiguity (Figure 2b). Area and major axis length are calculated based on an ellipse sharing the same second moment of the masked particles, providing an average 2D area and length distribution as shown in Figure 2c,d, respectively. The average area of the grain boundary precipitates is 3.87 μm² (std. dev. 5.06 μm²), and the average major axis length is 104 nm (std. dev. 114 nm), with the overall masked grain boundaries comprising 9.1% of the total field of view. Additionally, the elemental signals and weight fraction maps (Al, Ni, Er, Zr, Figure 2e) from STEM EDS are then averaged within each individual precipitate, resulting in a distribution of relative concentrations (Figure 2f). The average elemental ratios across all grain boundary precipitates as well as the matrix, are shown in Table S3 (Supporting Information). These results indicate that phases at the grain boundary are characterized by an increase in Ni and Er compared to the matrix, with the relative proportions consistent with enrichment of Ni and Er observed from APT results presented later in Figure 9 (i.e., the ternary phase). In addition, individually masked grain boundary precipitates from another dataset (Figure 3a; Figure S14, Supporting Information) acquired in a subregion of the sample reveal composition ratios with a wide range of variation within the

Figure 9. a) Reconstruction of LEAP dataset containing a fine dispersion of Zr-rich precipitates. The isosurfaces where Zr = 3.0% are shown in blue. b) Elemental segregation of Er and Ni to a large secondary phase. c) Proximity histogram for isosurface interfaces where Zr = 3.0%, showing enrichment in both Zr and Er. d) Proximity histogram for isosurface interfaces where Ni = 2.5%, showing enrichment in Ni, Er, and Zr.

overall distribution from Figure 2f. Compared to the matrix, or region 1 from Figure S14 (Supporting Information), the grain boundary precipitates are significantly enriched in Er and Ni and slightly deficient in Zr.

The STEM-HAADF image of one of the precipitates located at the grain boundary (Figure 3a,b) shows two distinct structures: the predicted Al-Ni-M ternary phase and Al-Zr/Er L1₂ phase (Figure 3b,c). Here, the region corresponding to the ternary phase contains Ni- and Er-rich atomic planes, while the L1₂ region is primarily Er- and Zr-rich (Figure 3d). In addition to the grain boundary precipitates, the presence of ordered nanoclusters within the matrix is observed in an atomic resolution HAADF image (Figure 3e). These nanoclusters are characterized by ordered atom column intensities on adjacent planes (Figure 3f), reflecting preferential occupancy of high and low atomic number elements consistent with L1₂-type ordering.[26]

To quantify the length scale of this ordering, the individual atom column intensities are normalized with respect to their respective eight nearest neighbors, resulting in a normalized intensity map highlighting local order (Figure 3g). Across multiple datasets, this statistically significant L1₂-type order is identified using kernel correlation and spatial autocorrelation,[27,28] yielding a Getis-Ord Gi* statistic order metric (Figure 3h). The Gi* statistic identifies hot spots and uses a distance-based comparison with neighbors to determine statistical significance with respect to the null hypothesis of no clustering (ordering).[29] After additional segmentation (Figure 3i), the average area and radii (approximated using a circle of equal area) are computed and shown in Figure 3j; Figure S21 (Supporting Information), respectively, for these short-range L1₂ nanoclusters. Overall, the ordered nanoclusters have an average area of 1.90 nm² (std. dev. 1.79), radius of 0.70 nm (std. dev. 0.35 nm), and area fraction of 17%.

Atomic-resolution STEM-HAADF images of one of the 1-5 nm precipitates more clearly show ordered bright and dim  $ \{110\} $ planes, corresponding with L1 $ _{2} $ coherent precipitates (Figure 3l). The Fourier transform of the image shows superlattice reflections consistent with an L1 $ _{2} $ structure, and atomic-resolution EDS maps indicate the segregation of Al versus Er/Zr in the L1 $ _{2} $ phase (Figure 3k–m). The composition of these ordered nanoclusters within the matrix is also evaluated using STEM EDS across the matrix (including L1 $ _{2} $ nanoclusters) as well as for an individual ordered cluster (Figure S19a,b, Supporting Information), respectively. The average overall matrix composition ratio is observed to be consistent with lower magnification STEM EDS results (Figure 2; Figure S14, Supporting Information). In contrast, the L1 $ _{2} $ nanoclusters with the matrix that are identified by the ordered atom column intensities in HAADF are characterized by a significant local increase in Zr and Er.

APT results taken from the grain interior clearly confirm the  $  \mathrm{L1}_{2} \mathrm{Al}_{3}(\mathrm{Er},\mathrm{Zr})  $ precipitation, corresponding with the  $ \approx 1-5 $ nm  $  \mathrm{L1}_{2}  $ precipitates seen from STEM characterization (Figure 9a,c). This size scale meets the requirement of starting finer than the optimum particle diameter of 17 nm for creep resistance. The APT of a sample containing ternary phase also confirms a composition near the predicted  $  \mathrm{Al}_{23} \mathrm{Ni}_{6} \mathrm{M}_{4}  $ ternary compound (Figure 9b,d).

In summary, there are currently several options for AM Al alloys with strength and ductility similar or higher than wrought Al 7075. $ ^{[30-33]} $ The design in this study was also upgraded and higher performances were achieved, such as as-built ductility of 19% with only 25% sacrifice in as-built strength (from 372 to 279 MPa). $ ^{[19]} $ The strength was also significantly increased beyond wrought Al 7075 by integration of other strengthening strategies. It is noted that this study holds the core idea of designing coarsening-resistant small precipitates to achieve high strength mainly via precipitation strengthening and by exploiting rapid solidification, and is the base for our other improved designs.

Going forward, submicron-meter scale precipitation of metastable phases can be employed to enhance the strength of other alloys, such as Ni-based superalloys, as demonstrated here for the candidate printable Al alloys. Moreover, as mentioned, precipitation strengthening can be combined with other strengthening mechanisms to achieve further strengthening, and the methodology can be tailored to maximize other mechanical properties such as ductility or creep resistance. The developed hybrid computational workflows can be extended to various multi-objective dilute and multi-component alloy

design scenarios, incorporating other microstructural features contributing to properties and printability, including manufacturing constraints, economic and environmental implications of the alloy composition, and employing accelerated experimental workflows. $ ^{[34,35]} $ This is especially valuable to leverage the inherent non-equilibrium conditions imposed by rapid solidification in AM, and the opportunity provided by AM for spatially tailored microstructure and multi-material component design in concert with structural optimization methods. $ ^{[36-38]} $ The algorithms can be enhanced to integrate experimental uncertainty and to incorporate data sources from experiments, simulations, and literature. Future deployment of interactive alloy design frameworks, such as via multi-agent models $ ^{[39,40]} $ alongside the use of automation in experimental validation, will further accelerate the design cycle of structural alloys for AM as well as traditional manufacturing for various industrial applications.

## 3. Methods Section

Precipitation Strengthening: In the Orowan strengthening mechanism, the shear stress required for looping around the precipitate was inversely proportional to the distance between precipitates  $ \lambda $ (see Equation 1). In this equation, M was the Taylor factor; G and  $ \nu $ were the shear modulus and Poisson ratio, respectively; b was the magnitude of Burgers’ vector; and  $ \bar{R} $ was the mean planar precipitate radius. Both  $ \bar{R} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \langle R \rangle $ and  $ \bar{\lambda} = (\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{4f}} - 1) \bar{R} $ depend on the mean radius  $ \langle R \rangle $. Here, f was the volume fraction of the precipitates. Therefore, under a certain precipitate volume fraction, the Orowan strength decreases significantly as the precipitate size increases.

 $$ \sigma_{\mathrm{or}}=M\frac{0.4Gb}{\pi\sqrt{1-v}}\cdot\frac{\ln\left(\frac{2\bar{R}}{b}\right)}{\bar{\lambda}} $$ 

Microstructure Stability at High Temperatures (Precipitate Coarsening Rates): The sample with minimized coarsening of precipitates maintains the strengthening at higher temperatures.[12] The kinetics of this process was controlled by the volume diffusion of solute elements in small precipitates to adjacent larger ones. Therefore, the coarsening rate depends first on whether the alloying elements in the matrix and precipitates were contributing to bulk diffusion, and second, on how the interface between precipitate and matrix allows this growth of precipitates. The interfacial contribution depends on the interfacial energy. Equation 2 explains the contribution of the bulk diffusion resistivity (denominator) and interfacial energy (numerator) to the coarsening rate in multi-component alloying systems[12] In this equation, the mobility matrix (M) was assumed to be diagonal. The1/M, were thus the inverse of diagonal coefficients.  $ \sigma $ was the interfacial energy of precipitate-matrix boundaries, and  $ V_{m}^{\beta} $ was the partial atomic volume.  $ \bar{C}_{i}^{\beta} - \bar{C}_{i}^{\alpha} $ refers to the temporal average solubility difference of the elements in the precipitates and the matrix at equilibrium and target temperature.

 $$ k\propto\frac{V_{m}^{\beta}\sigma}{\sum_{i=2}^{N}\left(\bar{C}_{i}^{\beta}-\bar{C}_{i}^{\alpha}\right)^{2}/M_{i}} $$ 

In this manuscript,  $ \sum_{i=2}^{N}(\bar{C}_{i}^{\beta}-\bar{C}_{i}^{\alpha})^{2}/M_{i} $ was referred to as diffusion resistivity. Reducing the mismatch of the lattice parameters between a matrix and precipitates was crucial for minimizing the interfacial energy between the phases in both coherent and semi-coherent boundaries. The absolute lattice parameter mismatch for the precipitate and matrix structures was calculated by Equation 3:

 $$ \varepsilon=100\%\left|1-\frac{a}{a_{0}}\right| $$ 

Here, a was a lattice parameter for the precipitate and  $ a_{0} $ was the Al matrix lattice parameter. In fact, the lattice parameters depend on both the alloying elements and the thermal expansion resulting from increasing at the target service temperature. The product of misfit strain ( $ \epsilon $) and the inverse of diffusion resistivity was considered as the coarsening metric.

Printability: The FR was the interval between the temperature at which the alloy was completely liquid and the temperature at which it was 99% solidified ( $ FR = T_{0}^{S} - T_{0.99}^{S} $). As this temperature interval gets larger, the dendrites were not fed with liquid and leads to shrinkage and thermal contraction, and finally cavities and hot cracking. Therefore, minimizing FR was essential to evaluate the HCS. Here, HCS of the developed Al alloy was analyzed by the model developed by Clyne and Davis to evaluate the CSC. $ ^{[15]} $ According to this model, mass and liquid feeding start from a normalized time  $ t_{R} $ (0.6–0.1 volume fraction of liquid) in which stresses at the mushy zone can be relaxed. However, the cracking-vulnerable time  $ t_{v} $ was defined from 0.1–0.01. Consequently, the ratio  $ t_{v}/t_{R} $ results in the CSC parameter. The data for the FR and CSC parameters was obtained by conducting nonequilibrium Scheil solidification simulations. To measure the CSC parameter, the heat flow was assumed to be proportional to the square root of time. Note that the high-melting precipitation of rare earth elements significantly increases the FR. However, based on experiments, these initial precipitations do not increase HCS. Thus, these precipitates were ignored during the FR and CSC calculations. Therefore, to ensure printability of the Al alloy, the HCS, which was the product of FR and CSC must be minimized. $ ^{[11]} $

ML Analysis: Using the LHS technique, 250 000 and 500 000 data were generated. Thermo-Calc was then used to perform single equilibrium calculations at 250 °C.[41] The concentrations of the elements in L1₂ phases and Al matrix were extracted using the TC-python interface and fed into Equation (2). The concentration of the five elements in the composition thus constitutes the feature/input vector. The input values were normalized by deducing the mean and scaling to the variance. Cuml library from RAPIDS[42] developed by NVIDIA was used for the KNN, RF, SVR, GB, and LR techniques, and TensorFlow was used for the NN regression technique. The data was divided into training (varying %) and test datasets 20%. The hyperparameters associated with the ML techniques were presented in Table S1 (Supporting Information). They were optimized using grid search and 5-fold cross-validation (80% for training and 20% for validation) of the RMSE using gradient descent and Adam optimizers.[43] The target predicted value was the coarsening metric of L1₂ phases. After defining the optimum set of hyperparameters for each technique, the prediction error was measured on the test data set for

different percentages of the training data. NN regression showed the minimum RMSE compared to other techniques at different percentages. The measurement was repeated five times for various initial seeds for NN, and the minimum and maximum of the RMSE were shown in Figure S2 (Supporting Information) with error bars. The lower RMSE of NN was partially due to the fact that the available hyperparameters in Cuml 3.2 were limited, and the whole spectrum of hyperparameters was not fully implemented. The NN regressor was thus used to forward predict coarsening metrics used in inverse design techniques. Two inverse design approaches were applied. First, BO with Gaussian process as a surrogate model was used, and the data was generated using the NN regressor. As a second method in this approach, the labels of the data were also directly calculated using the TC-python interface of Thermo-Calc. In the second approach, PSO was used for the inverse design technique, and the labels of data were generated from the NN regressor. Gaussian process was chosen as the surrogate model for the BO as it has a relatively low number of hyperparameters, the objective function was continuous, and the uncertainty of the fitted model was known. The uncertainty defines the tradeoff between exploration and exploitation in the acquisition function of BO.

The first strategy for finding the optimum design was mentioned in the main text, and the two other design strategies and the outcomes were mentioned here. The second strategy was to consider printability metrics as the main design objectives. CSC, FR, and HCS were thus minimized (Figure S9, Supporting Information). When evaluating the suggested designs of these iterations, it was seen that the suggested designs were expected to have poor mechanical performance, with higher priority given to features that reduce the metrics with the help of eutectic phases as opposed to strong, stable alloys.  $ Al_{3}Ni $ typically develops in a eutectic manner near the end of solidification, which enhances printability. However, this phase was soft and rapidly coarsens; thus, it reduces the strength at high temperatures.

For the third strategy of design, several printability metrics were combined with the high mechanical performance targets. For example, the objectives were minimization of  $ (CSC^* = CSC \times Coarsening\ metric) $,  $ (FR^* = FR \times Coarsening\ metric) $, and maximization of  $ (1-CSC) / Coarsening\ metric $. The suggested designs for multiple iterations of BO for objectives “CSC*” and “FR*” were presented in Figure S9 (Supporting Information).

Exploring the properties of the designs from these explorations and comparing them to the ML-optimized alloy, showed that, while there were some designs that initially seemed viable (with low coarsening metrics and high  $ L1_{2} $ phase percentage),  $ L1_{2} $ phases were developed during Scheil solidification at high fractions. Furthermore, these alloys have high fractions of adverse phases, such as  $ Al_{3}Ni $,  $ Al_{3}Y $ and  $ Al_{3}Zr $, which were connected to poor mechanical performance.  $ Al_{3}Y $ can form in a brittle structure (such as the  $ D0_{19} $ structure). It can also increase the volume percentage of  $ L1_{2} $ phases as it contributes to increasing metastable phases. However, Y-rich  $ L1_{2} $ phases have high a coarsening metric due to the high misfit strain; thus, the total phase percentage must be minimized. It was concluded that guidelines to improve printability could be discovered; however, when printability was considered in the objective functions, the mechanical performance significantly sacrifices.

SEM Characterization: SEM-BSE and SEM-EDS data were collected on a JEOL JSM7900F FE-SEM equipped with an Oxford Ultim Max 100 mm² detector. BSE images were collected at 5 kV and 20 kV to optimize surface sensitivity and contrast, respectively. SEM-EDS data were also collected at 5 kV and 15–20 kV, in order to prioritize spatial resolution of surface features at the lower voltage, and to permit detection of the full suite of elements, including Er at the higher voltage. EDS spectra and maps were collected and processed using Aztec 5.1 software.



SEM-BSE and SEM-EDS data of zero-nickel and induction-melted alloys were collected on a ThermoFisher Apreo HiVac SEM equipped with an EDAX Elite 150 SDD EDS detector. The BSE-EDS data were obtained at 15 kV to measure the presence of all elements and to give the optimal spatial resolution on EDS maps. The EDS spectra and maps were processed by EDAX APEX software. SEM-EBSD data of the top and side surface of the designed alloy were collected on a ThermoFisher Apreo HiVac SEM equipped with an EDAX Hikari EBSD detector. The EBSD data were collected at 15 kV, and the step size was set to 0.045  $ \mu $m to secure the spatial resolution. The raw Kikuchi diffractograms were post-analyzed in OIM and home-developed SphInx softwares. $ ^{[44]} $

STEM Characterization: Standard lift-out TEM samples were prepared using a Helios Nanolab 660 focused ion beam microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific). After selecting a sample region containing a representative collection of features of interest via backscatter electron imaging, a lamella was extracted and attached it to a molybdenum Omniprobe TEM grid using an Omniprobe 400 micromanipulator (Oxford Instruments). Due to the large ion range of Ga ions in aluminum, which can cause significant damage and corresponding artifacts during TEM imaging, the ion beam energy was reduced from 30 to 16 kV at a sample thickness of 300 nm, and then it was halved again at corresponding half thicknesses. Final polishing was performed at 750 V using a Model 1040 NanoMill (Fischione Instruments) until a thickness of ≈50 nm was reached. To remove the effects of Ga-ion damage, the TEM lamella was further cleaned using a Fischione 1051 TEM Mill. Light Ar-ion milling was performed at 0.3 and 0.1 kV for 3 and 1 min, respectively.

STEM images were captured with a probe-corrected Thermo Fisher Scientific Themis Z 60–300 kV probe aberration-corrected TEM/STEM using an accelerating voltage of 300 kV, a beam current of 40 pA, and a probe convergence semi-angle of 18.8 mrad. HAADF images were collected with a collection semi-angle range of 78–200 mrad. Atomic resolution HAADF images were drift-corrected using the Revolving STEM (RevSTEM) method, in which 8-frame image series were acquired with a 90° rotation between each consecutive frame.[45] Position-averaged convergent beam electron diffraction (PACBED) patterns were used to determine the sample thickness, which was ≈30 nm for the imaged region.[46] EDS was performed using a Thermo Fisher Scientific Super X detector and processed using the Thermo Fisher Scientific Velox software. Low magnification EDS was performed using a beam current of 200 pA and filtered using a 5 px averaging filter. Atomic-resolution EDS maps were acquired using a beam current of 50 pA and filtered using non-local principal component analysis (NLPCA).[47]

APT of 3D-Printed Samples: Specimens for APT were prepared following standard lift-out methods, using a FEI Helios 660

dual-beam FIB/SEM. APT was performed with a Cameca LEAP 4000 HR, operated in voltage-pulsing mode with the following experimental conditions: base temperature 40 K, pulse rate 100 kHz, pulse fraction 15%, and detection rate 0.5%.

Each LEAP dataset was reconstructed and analyzed using Cameca IVAS software version 3.6.14. SEM images of each LEAP tip were used to measure the shank half angle and tip radius; these images were incorporated into the shape of the reconstruction. Two of the LEAP tips showed a fine dispersion of Zr-rich precipitates, and one LEAP tip showed a large phase rich in Er and Ni. The Zr-rich precipitates were segmented using isosurfaces set to 3.0% Zr (Figure 9a). The Er- and Ni-rich phase was segmented using an isosurface set to 2.5% Ni, as shown in Figure 9b. The reconstructions shown here illustrate the Zr = 3.0% isosurfaces and the collection of Er and Ni in second phases, respectively. A proximity histogram concentration profile was applied to both second phases and for all interfaces set by the isosurfaces (Figure 9c,d). A bin size of 0.1 nm was used for both histograms.

The composition of the matrix and fine precipitates was measured using two LEAP tips segmented with Zr = 0.3 at.% isosurfaces, and the composition of the large second phase was measured from the third LEAP tip (Table S2, Supporting Information). These measurements indicate that the fine precipitates were the L1₂ phase, and the large second phase was the ternary Al-Ni-Er/Zr phase.

CT-Scan of Laser-Scanned Arc-Melted Samples: Micro-CT scans were conducted on a Zeiss Xradia Crystal CT system. Samples were mounted on a clip stage and positioned 21 mm from the X-ray source and 565 mm from the detector, allowing for full-volume scans of each  $ 5 \times 6 \times 2 $ mm sheet ample at 1.5  $ \mu $m voxel resolution. Scans were performed at 60 kV and 5 W with an LE2 filter, and each scan consisted of 2401 projections. Reconstruction was carried out using the Zeiss Scout-and-Scan Control System, and 3D image analysis was performed using ORS Dragonfly. To ensure reliable segmentation, the periphery of the volume was cropped to mitigate high X-ray transmission effects, and segmentation thresholds were selected based on the intensity contrast between pores and matrix. The porosity volume fraction was calculated, and pore morphology was characterized using aspect ratio (short-axis/long-axis length), that was automatically computed by the software.

Code Availability: The in-house code for hybrid ML/CALPHAD simulations has restrictions for accessibility due to a utility patent application. The CALPHAD part of the code was performed using Themo-Calc software, which was not publicly available. However, an essential part of the code, which works independently and presents library connections and design optimization, was provided on GitHub https://github.com/Taheri-Mousavi-Laboratory/BayesOpt-for-Alloy-Design. We used Python 3.11.7, Thermo-Calc 2024a, and BayBE 1.5.1.

# Additively Manufacturable High-Strength Aluminum Alloys with Coarsening-Resistant Microstructures Achieved via Rapid Solidification
S. Mohadeseh Taheri-Mousavi,* Michael Xu, Florian Hengsbach, Clay Houser, Zhaoxuan Ge, Benjamin Glaser, Shaolou Wei, Mirko Schaper, James M. LeBeau, Greg B. Olson, and A. John Hart*
Additively manufactured aluminum (Al) alloys with high strength have broad industrial applications. Strength promotion necessitates a high-volume fraction of small, closely spaced precipitates to effectively impede dislocation motion. Here, it is shown that for certain compositions in the Al-Er-Zr-Y-Yb-Ni alloy class, L1₂-Al₃M phases, the primary strength contributor, can initially precipitate as submicron-scale ( $ \approx $100 nm) metastable ternary phases under the rapid solidification of powder bed additive manufacturing; yet the subsequent coarsening-resistant L1₂-Al₃M phases that precipitate during heat treatment remain at the nanometer scale, imparting high strength. A candidate alloy is designed using hybrid calculation of phase diagrams (CALPHAD)-based integrated computational materials engineering (ICME) and Bayesian optimization algorithms. Powder is manufactured for this alloy and is additively manufactured into crack-free macroscale specimens with a strength that is five-fold that of the equivalent cast alloy and comparable to wrought Al 7075. After aging at 400 °C for 8 h, the room-temperature tensile strength reaches 395 MPa, which is 50% stronger than the best-known benchmark printable Al alloy. This integrated computational-experimental workflow shows the considerable potential to exploit rapid solidification in additive manufacturing to design alloys with commercially deployable properties.
## 1. Introduction
Additively manufactured (AM) aluminum (Al) alloys with high strength at elevated service temperatures (e.g.,  $ \approx $150–400 °C) can find broad industrial applications in components of jet engines, combustion engines, vacuum pumps, and more. $ ^{[1]} $ Laser-based AM, particularly laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) that operates under non-equilibrium conditions due to rapid solidification, can endow the alloy products with microstructural features that are challenging to attain via conventional near-equilibrium liquid metallurgy routes. One of the outstanding benefits of rapid solidification is the metastable phases that can effectively modulate the macroscopic physical and/or mechanical properties of as-built or heat-treated alloys.

Al-Ce-Mg alloys promotes a metastable ternary  $ Al_{23}CeMg_{6} $ phase, leading to higher strength.[3] Similar mechanical benefits have also been reported for  $ \theta^{\prime} $- $ Al_{2}Cu $ in cast and AM Al-Cu-Mn-Zr alloys.[4] In Al 2024, metastable  $ L1_{2} $- $ Al_{3}Ti $ due to rapid solidification, instead of stable  $ D0_{22} $- $ Al_{3}Ti $, leads to improvement in both strength and ductility.[5] Similarly, metastable  $ L1_{2} $- $ Al_{3}Zr $ instead of brittle  $ D0_{23} $- $ Al_{3}Zr $ in Al-Mg-Zr alloys induces noticeable precipitation strengthening as well as promoting grain refinement and inhibiting solidification cracking.[6] Finally, in AM Al-Ce-Mn alloys ( $ Al_{20}Mn_{2}Ce $),  $ Al_{6}Mn $ increases as-built hardness and transforms to  $ Al_{11}Ce_{3} $ and  $ Al_{12}Mn $ after aging, contributing to further increased hardness.[7] These and other examples show that rapid solidification can be exploited to trigger precipitation of metastable phases to unravel new routes for alloy design.
Three microstructural conditions are critical in designing Al alloys with high mechanical strength at elevated temperatures.
1) Thermodynamic stability over a broad temperature window. Two strategies are commonly employed to strengthen Al alloys $ ^{1} $: employing precipitates, especially with L1 $ _{2} $ structures, is the first main strategy, $ ^{[8]} $ and segmenting grains by laminated eutectic phases to further impede dislocation motion $ ^{1} $. Following the first strategy that is the focus of this study, strength has an inverse relationship with the precipitate size based on Orowan strengthening theory. $ ^{[8]} $ Thus, for a given volume fraction of precipitates, smaller precipitate size and spacing more effectively impede dislocation motion. Although laser AM generally leads to smaller precipitates due to short growth time during rapid solidification, precipitate sizes usually grow to the micrometer scale, especially as their volume fraction increases. Here, we show that for tailored compositions of Al alloys, rapid solidification can cause precipitation of metastable ternary phases at submicron-meter scales (Figures 1–3). The subsequent heat treatment of printed material transforms ternary phases into L1 $ _{2} $-Al $ _{3} $M (referred as L1 $ _{2} $ in this paper) and Al $ _{3} $Ni. Importantly, L1 $ _{2} $ phases remain at the nanometer scale even at high volume fractions.
2) Sluggish coarsening kinetics. Sluggish coarsening kinetics occur if the strengthening phases do not grow and the fraction of fast-growing phases is minimized. These two factors were considered in the present alloy design (Figure 4a,f, Results and Discussion: Step IV). To this end, L1₂ structures are preferred due to their coherent interfaces with the Al matrix. Certain elements in the periodic table promote L1₂ formation during casting or rapid solidification of AM. As discussed in Discussion S1 (Supporting Information), it is typically necessary to add heavy high-melting-point elements, which results in macro-segregation during casting. This engineering challenge thus limits the maximum concentration of each element. Layer-wise melting of laser AM, fortunately, circumvents this challenge.

Processing defects can be minimized by optimization of processing parameters. However, solidification defects originate from the solidification nature of the material, e.g., the time and temperature change, or the number of grain nucleation sources. For example, a widely used high-strength Al alloy, Al 7075, cannot readily be printed without modification to its composition as it is prone to hot cracking (Figure 5c; Figure S20, Supporting Information). $ ^{[9,10]} $
Through an integrated computational-experimental workflow, this study addresses the first two conditions and results in a family of alloys that do not exhibit hot cracking. To inhibit hot cracking in the sample, the temperature range and solidification time of the designed alloy were controlled. As the dimensions of the compositional space (here, five dimensions) and the number of objectives increase, defining the critical parameters governing the microstructural features and, ultimately, the desired engineering properties becomes significantly challenging, motivating the use of machine learning (ML) techniques (Figure 5a; Figure S1, Supporting Information). ## 2. Results and Discussion
CALPHAD-based ICME simulations were combined with ML techniques to efficiently explore the design space and find non-linear correlations among elemental concentrations (Figure 5b). Predicted mechanical properties were compared to the best-known benchmark Al alloy, which has been designed within the same compositional space and target properties, yet without ML techniques. For initial rapid validation of the designed compositions, promising candidates were induction melted and laser scanned to mimic the conditions of AM without requiring custom powder formulation. The top candidate alloy, Al-0.4Er-1Zr-1.33Ni (Al-2.33Er-3.19Zr-2.73Ni wt.%), was found to have 50% higher hardness (147 HV) after aging at 400 °C for 8 h than the benchmark alloy (100 HV for ‘alloy 1’, Al-1.35Er -1.15Zr-0.25Y-0.73Yb-2.14Ni, wt.%), which was prepared by the same method (Figure 1). The microstructure and properties of two other control alloys were also demonstrated to elucidate the design concept. As seen in Figure 1, slower solidification via induction melting (casting) leads to micro-meter scale ternary and Al₃M phases (Figure S15, Supporting Information) and 80% lower hardness (40 HV) than the 3D-printed sample (200 HV in Figure S17, Supporting Information).
An Al alloy with an identical molar percentage of Er and Zr but without Ni was also made. In this alloy  $ L_{1} $ phases directly form during rapid solidification. The hardness of this control sample was measured (W/O ternary in Figure 1), and its microstructure was analyzed (yellow arrow in Figure 1; Figure S16, Supporting Information). These clusters are not present in the top-designed alloy. This composition has a hardness of 110 HV, which is 34% lower than the designed composition containing submicron-meter scale ternary phases processed in an identical procedure.
Powder was then produced from the alloy that exhibited the highest hardness, and specimens were fabricated by LPBF AM, enabling hardness measurements, tensile tests, and microstructure characterization to be performed. After aging (8 h, 400 °C), hardness (200 HV) is once more 50% higher than the benchmark alloy (133 HV), and comparable to wrought Al 7075 (Figure S17, Supporting Information). The high strength and the coarsening resistance of the designed alloy at elevated temperatures were further confirmed by performing room temperature tensile tests on as-built and aged samples (Figure 6). The peak tensile strength after 8 h of aging is 395 MPa, which is likewise ≈50% higher than the benchmark printable Al alloy $ ^{11} $ (275 HV) and is maintained without significant decrease after aging for 48 h at 400 °C. In what follows, the design procedure for these alloys and then a reflection on further improvements in properties achieved by refinement of the design are described.
##### Step I: Extracting microstructural features
To realize an alloy composition that exhibits high-temperature strength and printability, microstructural features controlling these properties were first identified. During solidification of the Al alloy system (Al-Er-Zr-Y-Yb-Ni), several precipitates form, including D0₂₃-Al₃Zr (referred as Al₃Zr in this paper), L1₂, Al₃Ni, and metastable ternary phase Al₂₃Ni₆M₄. Al₃Zr is brittle and undermines strength and ductility;¹¹] its lower solvus temperature and phase percentage minimize this phase. These microstructural features can be quantified through single equilibrium calculations (Figure 4d,e). L1₂ is stable at high temperature (Figure S3, Supporting Information) and contributes to strengthening by impeding dislocations.[⁸] At low temperature, based on the Orowan strengthening mechanism, dislocations bow around precipitates and leave behind dislocation loops. Based on Equation (1) (see Methods Section, σ_or ∝  $ \frac{1}{R} $), the shear stress required for these mechanisms is inversely proportional to the precipitate size R, above the shear to bypass critical size of ≈2 nm. The goal is to achieve such a nanometer-scale dispersion after aging, and at elevated service temperature. A high content of L1₂ phase at 250 °C without formation during solidification (low L1₂ phase percentage at 660 °C, which is the melting temperature of pure Al) maximizes the concentration of nanoscale precipitates. These percentages were determined through single equilibrium calculations (Figure 4b,c). It is noteworthy that metastable precipitates such as Al₂₃Ni₆M₄, in which M can potentially be Er, Zr, Y, or Yb, may form at sub-micrometer scales during rapid solidification, working as efficient reactant reservoirs for the L1₂ precipitates during aging. The volume fractions of these precipitates after solidification must be maximized and are calculated from non-equilibrium Scheil solidification simulations (Figure 4f).
